Rana Raj Singh

[Rajput History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/rajput-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Rana Raj Singh

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Oct 25, 2025

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

6

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

Rana Raj Singh I of Mewar fiercely resisted Aurangzeb’s imperial aggression, forging strategic alliances and defending Hindu traditions with remarkable resolve. His reign (1652–1680) is remembered for both military valor and principled diplomacy.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6zzl!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0148f627-8b6a-495a-8bd6-4a7a03a0d7be_330x491.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6zzl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0148f627-8b6a-495a-8bd6-4a7a03a0d7be_330x491.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Background**

After Rana Amar Singh, the son of Maharana Pratap, abdicated the throne, it was his son Karan Singh II, who took over. His reign between 1620-1628 marked a rare interlude of peace in Mewar’s turbulent history, allowing for architectural renewal, diplomatic finesse, and cultural restoration—including the renovation of the famed Jain temples at Ranakpur.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R8xk!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff4123359-b4af-4412-bf54-6838750dd982_1024x640.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R8xk!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff4123359-b4af-4412-bf54-6838750dd982_1024x640.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Rana Karan Singh II’s reign (1620–1628) was marked by visionary water engineering and architectural patronage, including stormwater ditches around Lake Pichola, the dome of Jagmandir, Gol Mahal, and a tank at Krishna Niwas.

This system not only prevented flooding but also **enabled irrigation**, turning seasonal rains into agricultural sustenance.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AQBY!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9b1261aa-0169-4196-a57b-ace8663700d6_1024x678.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AQBY!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9b1261aa-0169-4196-a57b-ace8663700d6_1024x678.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!f2An!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F76438bf5-5e7b-4db4-a88b-f6530652cc7a_1024x768.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!f2An!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F76438bf5-5e7b-4db4-a88b-f6530652cc7a_1024x768.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

His succcesor Jagat Singh I completed the majestic Jag Mandir Palace and attempted to rebuild Chittorgarh Fort’s defenses, but this violated a Mughal treaty—prompting Emperor Shahjahan to dispatch Saidullah Khan against Mewar.

Jagat Singh I’s reign was a balancing act between **cultural assertion and political caution**. While he beautified Udaipur and reinforced Mewar’s identity through architecture, his attempt to reclaim Chittorgarh’s legacy revealed the **limits of Rajput sovereignty** under Mughal oversight.

Raj Singh was the eldest son of *Rana Jagat Singh I* and *Maharani Medtaniji* (also known as Jana Devi), daughter of Raj Singh Mertia Rathore. He ascended the throne of Mewar in 1652 after the death of his father, beginning a reign that would span nearly three decades.

Upon his coronation, Raj Singh performed the sacred *Tula Daan*—donating his weight in precious gems and stones at the revered *Shree Eklingnath ji Temple*, symbolizing his devotion and generosity. He was known for *donating gold, silver, and precious metals* to the needy, and for initiating *numerous welfare projects* that uplifted the people of Mewar.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Year!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2a23b354-8e17-42e2-afc2-db138a157b54_1024x768.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Year!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2a23b354-8e17-42e2-afc2-db138a157b54_1024x768.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In the mid-17th century, Mewar faced recurring droughts that devastated agriculture and livelihoods. In response, **Rana Raj Singh I commissioned the construction of Rajsamand Lake**, located near the town of Rajnagar in present-day Rajasthan.

The lake was built across the Gomati River, with inflows from the Kelwa and Tali rivers. The **main dam was completed on June 26, 1670**, though some sources note final touches continuing until 1676. Over **60,000 workers** were employed, making it one of the largest public works projects of its time. This not only alleviated famine-related distress but also **revived the local economy** through wages and trade.

He followed his ancestor’s traditions of building lakes across Mewar, which also served **ritual and cultural functions**, often surrounded by ghats, temples, and pavilions that reinforced it’s sacred geography.

In 1669, Mughal emperor **Aurangzeb issued a farman to demolish major Hindu temples**, targeting sacred Vaishnava sites in **Mathura, Vrindavan, and Gokul**. Among the endangered icons was the revered **Shrinathji**, a child form of Krishna worshipped at **Govardhan Hill** near Mathura.

To protect the deity, Vaishnava devotees first moved the idol to **Agra**, where it remained hidden for nearly six months.While many rulers hesitated, **Rana Raj Singh I of Mewar boldly offered sanctuary**, vowing to protect the idol even at the risk of imperial wrath.The idol’s journey to safety took **32 months**, traversing hostile terrain and political uncertainty.

As the cart carrying the idol reached a village called **Sihad**, its wheels **sank axle-deep into the eart,** a sign the priests interpreted as divine will. They declared this the chosen site for the deity’s new home.

In **1672**, under Raj Singh’s patronage, **Goswami Damodar Das Bairagi** oversaw the construction of the **Shrinathji Temple at Nathdwara**, which became a major pilgrimage center. The temple catalyzed a **Vaishnava cultural revival**, attracting artists, musicians, and poets. Nathdwara emerged as a hub of **Pushtimarg tradition**, with its iconic **Pichwai paintings** and devotional music.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r5-b!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3111aaa8-8fb5-401d-9005-59317c6edcbe_1024x512.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r5-b!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3111aaa8-8fb5-401d-9005-59317c6edcbe_1024x512.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AC_r!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6c37a47c-6c0c-418e-8e41-527a911f6f22_292x172.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AC_r!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6c37a47c-6c0c-418e-8e41-527a911f6f22_292x172.png)

Rana Raj Singh I remained neutral during the Mughal war of succession between Aurangzeb and Dara Shikoh, instead launching his own expansionist campaign under the guise of a “Tikadaur”—a ritual raid into enemy territory.

He chose **not to side with either**, recognizing that Mewar’s interests would not be served by entangling itself in a dynastic feud. His neutrality preserved Mewar’s autonomy and avoided unnecessary bloodshed.\\

He used the chaos to **launch his own campaign**, invoking the Rajput tradition of **“Tikadaur”**—a ceremonial raid into enemy territory, often symbolic but sometimes strategic. Under this pretense, he **reclaimed lost territories**, including **Mandalgarh, Jahazpur, and Banera**, which had previously fallen under Mughal control.

The Tikadaur allowed him to **assert Mewar’s sovereignty** without openly declaring war, maintaining the veneer of tradition while executing a shrewd territorial expansion.

In **1658**, he **raided Mughal outposts**, capturing **Mandal, Banera, Shahpura, and Sawar**, and imposed his own levies and taxes. He extended his campaign to **Malpura, Tonk, Chaksu, and Lalsot**, returning to Udaipur with spoils—**a symbolic assertion of sovereignty**.

In **1659**, he attacked **Dungarpur and Banswara**, both under Mughal control, and **brought them under Mewar’s dominion**.

**His son Kunwar Bhim Singh**, continued this aggressive posture, **capturing Idar** in Gujarat’s Sabarkantha district. He **plundered Mughal trading hubs** at **Ahmedabad and Vadnagar**, disrupting imperial commerce and asserting Rajput defiance.

In **1679**, Aurangzeb reimposed the **jizya tax on non-Muslims**, sparking widespread outrage. Raj Singh I **refused to pay**, declaring it unjust and oppressive.

His defiance was so legendary that **Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj taunted Aurangzeb**, saying:  
*“If you have any courage, collect jizya from Raj Singh of Mewar, instead of troubling poor Hindus.”*

Charumati, the sister of **Raja Man Singh of Kishangarh**, was renowned for her beauty and dignity. When **Aurangzeb**, known for his authoritarian rule and religious orthodoxy, saw her portrait or heard of her grace, he **demanded she be sent to his harem or married to him**—a fate many Rajput princesses feared under Mughal dominance.

Under pressure from the imperial court, Man Singh was forced to comply. But **Charumati refused**, declaring she would **rather die than marry Aurangzeb**, a powerful act of resistance that echoed the Rajput tradition of *honor over submission*.

Knowing that only **Rana Raj Singh I of Mewar** had the courage and military strength to defy Aurangzeb, Man Singh urged Charumati to **write a letter to Raj Singh**, pleading for protection.

Upon receiving Charumati’s letter, **Raj Singh responded with swift resolve**, mobilizing his forces and **challenging Aurangzeb’s demand**. His intervention not only **saved Charumati’s honor**, but also **reaffirmed Mewar’s role as a sanctuary for dharma and dignity**.

Some accounts suggest that **Raj Singh later married Charumati**, though this remains debated among historians. What is undisputed is his **defiance of imperial coercion** and his **protection of Rajput pride**.

In **1679**, the death of **Raja Jaswant Singh of Marwar** triggered a succession crisis. Aurangzeb, exploiting the moment, **refused to recognize Jaswant’s infant son Ajit Singh** as heir, instead attempting to annex Marwar into the Mughal Empire.

**Durgadas Rathore**, Jaswant’s loyal general, led a **guerrilla resistance**, smuggling Ajit Singh out of Delhi and rallying Rajput forces. This sparked the **Rajput War**, a prolonged conflict that lasted until **Aurangzeb’s death in 1707**, marked by skirmishes, sieges, and shifting alliances.

Also the famous story of Hadi Rani, occurred during Raj Singh’s reign, the wife of his commander, Ratan Singh Chundavat. She was the daughter of Sangram Singh of Bundi, her real name was Salah Kunwar. It’s believed that when Ratan Singh reluctant to go to the battle field, asked her to give something as a token of remembrance, she cut off her head and gave it to him. Her sacrifice moved Ratan Singh, who fell upon the enemy forces with a vengenance.

Rana Raj Singh I, the lion of Mewar, passed away on October 22, 1680 (not 1860), allegedly poisoned by his own men under orders from Aurangzeb, whose imperial ambitions he had long defied. His death marked the end of a reign that was as strategically astute as it was spiritually profound.

6
